title: Influence: The Psychology of Persuasion
author: Robert B. Cialdini
subtitle: The Psychology of Persuasion
authorBio: Robert B. Cialdini (born April 27, 1945) is Regents' Professor Emeritus of Psychology and Marketing at Arizona State University. He earned his PhD in social psychology from the University of North Carolina and completed postgraduate training at Columbia University. Cialdini is widely regarded as the foundational expert in the science of influence and persuasion, often called the 'Godfather of Influence.' His books have sold over seven million copies in 44 languages. He was elected to the National Academy of Sciences in 2019. He has advised Google, Microsoft, Coca-Cola, and both the Obama and Clinton presidential campaigns. He founded INFLUENCE AT WORK and later the Cialdini Institute to train professionals in ethical persuasion.
awards: Charlie Munger gave Cialdini a Berkshire Hathaway Class A share in appreciation
description: Robert Cialdini's landmark exploration of the psychology of compliance identifies six universal principles that drive human decision-making: reciprocation, commitment and consistency, social proof, liking, authority, and scarcity. Based on three years of undercover fieldwork in sales, fundraising, and telemarketing combined with decades of experimental research, the book reveals how compliance professionals trigger automatic, predictable responses. Each chapter blends controlled studies with vivid real-world case studies — from Hare Krishna募捐者的 flower tactic to the Tupperware party model — demonstrating how these principles operate in everyday life. The 2021 expanded edition adds a seventh principle, unity, reflecting the power of shared identity. Cialdini's ethical framework distinguishes honest persuasion (informing with relevant, truthful information) from manipulation (deceptive triggering of mental shortcuts).
excerpt: The definitive scientific exploration of why people say yes — and how to apply these insights ethically in business and everyday life.
keyIdeas: Reciprocation: The universal human obligation to repay — even unsolicited gifts create a sense of debt
Commitment and Consistency: Once a choice is made, people work to align subsequent behavior with that commitment to avoid cognitive dissonance
Social Proof: In ambiguous situations, we determine correct behavior by observing what similar others are doing
Liking: We comply with requests from people we like, which is triggered by similarity, compliments, familiarity, and association
Authority: Deference to perceived experts is automatic — titles, uniforms, and credentials can trigger compliance without critical evaluation
Scarcity: Opportunities seem more valuable when limited — the fear of losing an opportunity is a stronger motivator than the prospect of gain
Unity (2021): Shared identity — feel like 'we' rather than 'us vs. them' — amplifies all other principles
keyTakeaways: Most human decisions are automatic, triggered by specific features of a situation
Compliance professionals deliberately manipulate these triggers to secure agreement
Awareness of the principles is the first, but not sufficient, line of defense
Emotional arousal (feeling rushed, excited, or pressured) signals that a trigger may be in use
Ethical influence provides honest, relevant information that helps someone make a better decision

whoShouldRead: Marketing and sales professionals seeking evidence-based persuasion strategies
Entrepreneurs and business leaders who negotiate deals and lead teams
Anyone who has ever made a purchase they regretted and wants to understand why
Students of psychology, behavioral economics, and communication
Consumers who want to recognize and resist manipulative advertising tactics
Politicians, advocates, and campaigners who need to move people to action
whoShouldSkip: Readers looking for a purely philosophical or ethical examination of persuasion
Those who believe all influence is inherently coercive and should be opposed
bestsellerStatus: Warren Buffett: 'One of the best business books of all time'
